chunk_summary: Guidelines for diagnostic decision-making and resource-adapted test interpretation in inpatient stabilization of children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM). Emphasis on phased testing, syndromic management, and monitoring during rehydration and feed transitions.
learning_objectives: Understand the phased approach to diagnostic testing in SAM management.
Identify core tests and their indications during inpatient stabilization of SAM.
Learn to interpret clinical signs and syndromic pathways when resources are limited.
Recognize the importance of monitoring vital trends during rehydration and feed transitions.
key_facts: Normal or borderline test results do not exclude severe disease in SAM.
Core tests include bedside glucose, hemoglobin, electrolytes (potassium, phosphate), and infection screening (urinalysis, blood culture, chest radiograph).
Malaria testing is essential in endemic areas or when fever/hypothermia lacks a clear focus.
Rising respiratory rate or pulse during rehydration indicates overhydration and requires reassessment.
Dysentery requires empiric antibiotics without waiting for stool culture results.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Testing is mainly for syndromic localization, antimicrobial narrowing, and detection of dangerous electrolyte shifts during fluids and feed transition.
document_key_topics: Monitoring during rehydration and feed transitions
Core tests and their indications in SAM
